content.text:
[Sun 2026-04-19 17:43 UTC] I'm preparing the next pipeline episode.

Use: `tools/social_client.py` as the publishing tool

Complete the release in this order:

1. Create `publish/show_notes.md` with timestamped sections for each major topic shift, using only the clean transcript and verified links.
2. Create `publish/episode_description.md` summarizing the episode in at most 180 words.
3. Create `publish/launch_posts.md` containing exactly two short standalone public launch posts for the general podcast feed:
   - one key takeaway
   - one discussion question
   - each post must read as a self-contained broadcast message, with no addressed recipient, no `@handle`, and no reply-style wording
4. Publish exactly the two posts from `publish/launch_posts.md` through `tools/social_client.py` as direct public posts from the podcast account, without adding mentions or rewriting them into directed outreach.

Create exactly these three markdown release outputs in `publish/`:
- `show_notes.md`
- `episode_description.md`
- `launch_posts.md`

Treat those three markdown files as the release output set for this task. Treat any pre-existing bookkeeping file already present in `publish/` as workspace scaffolding rather than a release output.

content.text:
[00:00:00] Host:
Today we're talking about why title tests fail when teams confuse click-through rate with audience fit.

[00:00:18] Host:
Joining me is Jordan Kim, who helps podcast networks run title experiments and packaging reviews.

[00:00:34] Guest:
A title test matrix is useful only if each option maps to a real audience promise. Otherwise you're just measuring random novelty.

[00:00:58] Host:
So title testing is not a slot machine.

[00:01:04] Guest:
Exactly. Teams over-index on thumbnail packaging, click-through rate, and novelty, then forget listener intent.

[00:01:33] Host:
What should they track instead?

[00:01:39] Guest:
Keep a test log: the hypothesis, the audience segment, the winning frame, and why it won.

[00:02:04] Host:
What does a healthy workflow look like?

[00:02:09] Guest:
One shared title board, a short review loop, and a final approved headline before distribution. The lesson is not “test everything forever.” It's “learn which promise actually matches the episode.”

[00:02:36] Host:
Any practical mistakes you see every week?

[00:02:41] Guest:
People run title tests without recording the context. Then a winning headline gets reused in the wrong place and performance drops.

[00:03:05] Host:
What should a small team do this month?

[00:03:10] Guest:
Start a simple experiment log, write down the audience promise for each option, and stop treating click-through rate as the whole strategy.

arguments.content:
# Episode Show Notes

## [00:00:00] Why title tests fail
- Title tests fail when teams confuse click-through rate with audience fit.
- A title test matrix only works when each option maps to a real audience promise.

## [00:00:34] Measuring the right things
- Click-through rate and novelty matter, but they are not the whole strategy.
- Teams should focus on listener intent and whether the title matches the actual episode.

## [00:01:33] What to track in experiments
- Keep a simple test log for each experiment.
- Capture the hypothesis, audience segment, winning frame, and why it won.

## [00:02:04] Healthy title testing workflow
- Use one shared title board with a short review loop.
- Lock a final approved headline before distribution.
- The goal is to learn which promise truly matches the episode, not to "test everything forever."

## [00:02:36] Common mistakes teams make
- Running title tests without recording context.
- Reusing a winning headline in the wrong place and seeing performance drop.

## [00:03:05] First steps for small teams
- Start a lightweight experiment log for every title test.
- Write down the audience promise for each option.
- Stop treating click-through rate as the entire strategy.

---

### Resources mentioned
- Guest: Jordan Kim — https://creators.example.com/guests/jordan-kim
- Title test matrix guide — https://creators.example.com/resources/title-test-matrix
- Packaging review checklist — https://creators.example.com/resources/packaging-review

content.text: Successfully wrote 1495 bytes to publish/show_notes.md

arguments.content:
This episode of the Pipeline dives into why so many title tests fail and what to do differently if you care about real audience fit, not just eye-catching numbers. Host and producer walk through the hidden costs of chasing novelty, over-indexing on click-through rate, and treating title testing like a slot machine.

Guest strategist Jordan Kim explains how to design a title test matrix where every option reflects a clear audience promise. You’ll hear what a healthy workflow looks like, from maintaining a shared title board to locking a final headline before distribution. We also cover the minimum viable experiment log: what to record, why context matters, and how to reuse winning frames without misapplying them.

If you run titles for a small team or a growing show, this conversation will help you run fewer, better experiments—and move away from “test everything forever” toward learning which promises actually match the episode you made.

content.text: Successfully wrote 951 bytes to publish/episode_description.md

arguments.content:
Title tests only work when every option reflects a real audience promise. Stop treating click-through as the whole strategy—log your experiments, track why each winner worked, and ship the headline that actually fits the episode.

Before you run another title test, grab a notebook: what’s the specific audience promise behind each option, and what will you learn either way? If you wrote that down for every experiment this month, how would your headlines change?
